summary: This guest post reviews Daniel Koretz's book 'Measuring Up,' which provides a comprehensive critique of high-stakes standardized testing. The review highlights how test scores are samples from distributions rather than absolute measures, and how the push for 'reliability' creates trade-offs with test sophistication. Koretz documents the 'sawtooth pattern' where scores spike when new tests are introduced as teachers learn to game them, then plateau. The book argues that standards-based reporting (proficient vs. not) loses crucial information and relies on arbitrary cutoffs. The review concludes that high-stakes decisions about teachers based on just 2-3 years of test scores are fundamentally unreliable given the statistical noise involved.
key_points: Test scores are samples from a distribution, not absolute measures - taking the same test multiple times yields different results, and more sophisticated tests (free-form answers, experiments) introduce more variability
The 'sawtooth pattern' shows score inflation is inevitable: new test introduced → rapid score gains as gaming is learned → plateau → repeat with next test
The 'myth of the vanishing variance' exposes how goals like 'all students proficient in 4 years' imply impossible compression of score distributions never seen anywhere
Standards-based reporting (proficient/not proficient) loses information by collapsing scores into 4 arbitrary bins and obscuring within-bin variation
There's a fundamental tension: reliable tests are easier to game, while sophisticated 'tests worth teaching to' produce noisy results that make high-stakes teacher evaluation statistically dubious
money_quotes: a test result is really a sample from a distribution... and not an absolute measure of what someone knows

It sounds good to say that every kid who takes a certain test in four years will score as proficient, but that corresponds to a score distribution with much less variance than you'll ever see. Maybe we should stop lying to ourselves?
score inflation is real, pervasive, and unavoidable, unless we bite the bullet and do away with high-stakes tests
the more reliable tests, being more predictable, are probably easier to game
This noise is a huge deal when you realize that high-stakes decisions about teachers are made based on just two or three years of test scores
even standards-based reporting tends to slide back into comparisons: since we don't know exactly what proficient means, we're happiest when our school, or district, or state places ahead of others
key_insight: High-stakes standardized testing is caught in an impossible bind: reliable tests are gameable and narrow, while comprehensive tests produce noise too large for the consequential decisions being made based on them.
